content:
3 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When you’ve to search users by multiple filters, sort them, pick specific fields, and paginate.
Where does that query go?
In GET, it goes in the URL:
GET /users?filter[status]=active&filter[role]=admin&filter[created_after]=2026-01-01&sort=created_at:desc&fields=id,name,email,role&page=2&limit=50 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.comThat’s bad. Now add a full-text search or a nested JSON condition on top of that.
URLs have a size limit. And URLs get logged everywhere. Nginx, load balancers, CDNs. If your query has anything sensitive in it, it’s now sitting in your logs.
So you choose to use POST and move the query to the body:
POST /users/search HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json{
"filter": { "status": "active", "role": "admin", "created_after": "2026-01-01" },
"sort": { "created_at": "desc" },
"fields": ["id", "name", "email", "role"],
"page": 2,
"limit": 50
}This works. But it’s still wrong.
POST tells HTTP “something is changing”. A proxy sees it and assumes state is being modified. An HTTP client won’t auto-retry it if the connection drops. A cache won’t store the response.
You’re just reading data. Nothing is changing. But there’s no way to tell the protocol that.

The Fix: HTTP QUERY Method
RFC 10008 defines a new HTTP method: QUERY.
It works like POST. It takes a request body. But unlike POST, it tells HTTP this is a read-only operation. It can be retried automatically. The response can be cached.
Same request, now with QUERY:
QUERY /users HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json{
"filter": { "status": "active", "role": "admin", "created_after": "2026-01-01" },
"sort": { "created_at": "desc" },
"fields": ["id", "name", "email", "role"],
"page": 2,
"limit": 50
}The body is the same. But now every proxy, cache, and HTTP client knows this is a read.

What Makes QUERY Different
The RFC summarizes it cleanly:
GETQUERYPOSTSafe (read-only)YesYesNoCan be auto-retriedYesYesNoCacheableYesYesLimitedHas a request bodyNoYesYesHas a URI for the queryYesOptionalNo
QUERY sits right between GET and POST. Body like POST, behaviour like GET.
Content-Type is Required
Every QUERY request needs a Content-Type. No exceptions.
The server needs to know what format the query is in before it can do anything with it.

• No Content-Type: 400 Bad Request

• Format not supported: 415 Unsupported Media Type

• Query is valid format but makes no sense (like SQL pointing to a table that doesn’t exist): 422 Unprocessable Content

Does the Server Support QUERY?
Not every server supports it yet. You can check with OPTIONS:
OPTIONS /users HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.comHTTP/1.1 200 OK
Allow: GET, QUERY, HEAD, OPTIONSOr just send a QUERY and see what comes back. If the server doesn’t support it, you get 405 Method Not Allowed with an Allow header showing what it does support.

Using It in Python
Most Python HTTP clients support custom methods. This works right now.
Client with requests:
import requestsresponse = requests.request(
method="QUERY",
url="https://api.example.com/users",
json={
"filter": {"status": "active", "role": "admin"},
"sort": {"created_at": "desc"},
"fields": ["id", "name", "email"],
"page": 1,
"limit": 50
},
headers={"Accept": "application/json"}
)print(response.json())Server with FastAPI:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from pydantic import BaseModelapp = FastAPI()class UserQuery(BaseModel):
filter: dict
sort: dict
fields: list[str]
page: int = 1
limit: int = 50@app.api_route("/users", methods=["QUERY"])
async def query_users(request: Request):
body = await request.json()
query = UserQuery(**body)
results = fetch_users(query)
return {"results": results, "page": query.page}
Final Thoughts
GET breaks when queries get complex. POST works but it’s the wrong tool.
QUERY is exactly what was missing. A read operation with a body, that HTTP actually understands.
